text.fullText:
In my experience as a Broker, dispatcher, driver, fleet Manager and owner operator over the last 25 years I would disagree with the requirements for broker transparency. Carriers set the rate! Carriers are not forced to take any rate at any time! This is akin to making Walmart include their profit margin on every product on their shelf!
Many brokers bid, long-term contracts of quarterly, semi-annual or annual terms. In some parts (time) of the contract they may lose money, well in other parts (time) of the contract they may make over the standard margin on a particular transaction. This to provide consistent Freight spend cost for the client. However, they may maintain a standard margin throughout the term of the contract. It is NOT calculated on each transaction rather the term of the contract. A broker's outlook is not a single transaction rather a running total of transactions. Margins on a single transaction do not provide a clear picture of the of profitability.
Small and independent carriers have continually over leveraged and undersaved or planned for the cypical nature of freight. This failure of business savvy should not fall on the broker. A carrier is at no time forced to accept a rate as the carrier sets the rate. Carriers knowing their total cost per mile at which they achieve profitability is imperative. Also knowing the area in witch they are operating.
In other industries where brokers are used, a set point of commission is determined before the sale. However, none of these brokers are required by law to provide monetary guarantees for the transaction. Freight brokers act as the bank for the carriers in many instances. By guaranteeing payment to the carrier without a guarantee of payment from the shipper. This is an additional risk that other brokers and other industries do not have. This at many time comes at a cost and or loss to the broker.
Carriers must learn to work within the market just as any other business would have to adjust their practices, so she carriers.
I can think of no other industry where transparency of profit margin is required to be transparent! Should a carrier be required to discloses total operating cost and profits to shippers and brokers?
Why do truck stop fueling stations charge on average 50 cents more per a gallon for truck diesel as opposed to auto diesel? Are they not considered to be gouging motor carriers? Why do they not have to disclose the cost of their diesel and the profit margin? Is it because a free market allows a carrier to buy fuel wherever they want at whatever price they choose to pay? Why is this any different than a freight broker? At what point does transparency become intrusive to the business? Where do we draw the line. Do we have truck manufacturers disclose manufacturing cost as opposed to sales price? What if Peterbilt was making 150% over manufacturing costs on every sale of its truck? Should be require transparency as these also add to the cost of operation of a motor carrier? Large and mega carriers can negotiate this margin lower with truck manufacturers. However OOIDA's clients or small motor carriers to not possess this type of buying power.
Retail typically makes 100% margin on every item that they sell. If Walmart pays a dollar for a widget, they charge $2 for that same widget. You have a choice whether you want to buy that widget or not. You may use a credit card, a debit card or cash. But at no point in a free market is Walmart required? Transparency on how much that widget cost them.
Transparency for a single transaction only shows that single transaction, it is not a picture into the bigger business or the overall profitability of a contract. It is only that one transaction. A determination of business practices cannot be determine by viewing a single transaction!
Many small and independent motor carriers, including the ooida's clients have very little knowledge or understanding of the overall industry other than what it takes to operate their truck. Education into the broader view of the market and understanding how the market operates would go a long way for understanding real margin value and operating cost.
